title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 14 (Fragment 3)
text:
“Yes, Your Grace,” the man said feebly. They heard him scuttle away.

“And he’s sure I’m mad,” said the Duke. “Finished?”

Just then, Tonino’s words came loose, with a sort of jerk, and he knew the spell had worked in some way or another. “Yes,” he said.

But nothing seemed to happen. The Duke said philosophically that it would take a mouse quite a while to run from the Corso to the Palace, and strode on to the kitchens. They thought he was mad there, too, Tonino could tell. The Duke asked for two bread rolls and two pats of butter and solemnly put one into each pocket. No doubt they thought he was madder still when he remarked to no one: “There’s a cigar cutter in my right pocket that spreads butter quite well.”

“Indeed, Your Grace?” they heard someone say dubiously.

Just then, someone rushed in screaming about the griffins from the Piazza Nuova. They were flying across the river, straight for the Palace. There was a general panic. Everyone screamed and yammered and said it was an omen of defeat. Then someone else rushed in yelling that one griffin had actually reached the Palace and was sliding down the marble front. There was more outcry. The next thing, everyone said, the great gold Angel from the Cathedral would fly away too.

Tonino was taking advantage of the confusion to bash a piece off his roll with the Duke’s lighter, when the Duke bellowed, “Nonsense!” There was sudden quiet. Tonino dared not move, because everyone was certainly looking at the Duke.

“Don’t you see?” said the Duke. “It’s just an enemy trick. But we in Caprona don’t frighten that easily, do we? Here—you—go and fetch the Montanas. And you go and get the Petrocchis. Tell them it’s urgent. Tell as many of them to come as possible. I shall be in the North gallery.” And he went striding off there, while Angelica and Tonino jigged against bread and tried not to tread in the butter.

When he got to the gallery, the Duke sat down on a window seat. Angelica and Tonino stood half out of his pockets and managed to eat their bread and butter. The Duke amiably handed the cigar cutter from one to the other and, in between whiles, seemed lost in thought, staring at the white puffs of shells bursting on the hills behind Caprona.

Angelica was inclined to be smug. “I told you,” she said to Tonino, “my spells always work.”

“Iron griffins,” said Tonino, “aren’t mice.”

“No, but I’ve never done anything as big as that before,” said Angelica. “I’m glad it didn’t knock the Palace down.”

The Duke said gloomily, “The guns of Pisa are going to do that soon. I can see gunboats on the river, and I’m sure they aren’t ours. I wish your families would be quick.”

But it was half an hour before a polite footman came up to the Duke, causing him to flip his pocket flaps down and scatter buttery crumbs in all directions. “Your Grace, members of the Montana and Petrocchi families are awaiting you in the Large Reception Saloon.”

“Good!” said the Duke. He leaped up and ran so fast that Tonino and Angelica had to brace their feet on the seams of his pockets and hang on hard to the edges. They lost their footing several times, even though the Duke tried to help them by holding his pockets as he ran. They felt him clatter to a stop. “Blast!” he said. “This is always happening!”

vocabulary.definition: to shout loudly and powerfully
vocabulary.example: Tonino was taking advantage of the confusion to bash a piece off his roll with the Duke’s lighter, when the Duke bellowed, “Nonsense!

vocabulary.definition: to prepare for something difficult or to give support
vocabulary.example: He leaped up and ran so fast that Tonino and Angelica had to brace their feet on the seams of his pockets and hang on hard to the edges.

vocabulary.definition: to make a continuous rattling sound
vocabulary.example: They felt him clatter to a stop.

vocabulary.definition: an instance of being beaten in a battle or contest
vocabulary.example: Everyone screamed and yammered and said it was an omen of defeat.

vocabulary.definition: in a doubtful or suspicious manner
vocabulary.example: ” they heard someone say dubiously.

vocabulary.definition: a person or group that is hostile or opposed
vocabulary.example: “It’s just an enemy trick.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that lacks strength or force
vocabulary.example: “Yes, Your Grace,” the man said feebly.

vocabulary.definition: a male servant in a wealthy household
vocabulary.example:
”

vocabulary.example: But we in Caprona don’t frighten that easily, do we?

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows sadness or pessimism
vocabulary.example:
”

The Duke said gloomily, “The guns of Pisa are going to do that soon.

vocabulary.definition: a small device used to produce flame

vocabulary.definition: spoken or written words that have no meaning or make no sense
vocabulary.example: Tonino was taking advantage of the confusion to bash a piece off his roll with the Duke’s lighter, when the Duke bellowed, “Nonsense!

vocabulary.definition: a sign or event regarded as a portent of good or evil
vocabulary.example: Everyone screamed and yammered and said it was an omen of defeat.

vocabulary.definition: a strong protest or expression of anger or disapproval
vocabulary.example: There was more outcry.

vocabulary.definition: a sudden uncontrollable fear causing wild behavior
vocabulary.example: There was a general panic.

vocabulary.definition: in a calm and reasonable way, accepting problems without anxiety
vocabulary.example: The Duke said philosophically that it would take a mouse quite a while to run from the Corso to the Palace, and strode on to the kitchens.

vocabulary.definition: a short burst or cloud of smoke, air, or steam
vocabulary.example: The Duke amiably handed the cigar cutter from one to the other and, in between whiles, seemed lost in thought, staring at the white puffs of shells bursting on the hills behind Caprona.

vocabulary.definition: a large room used for receiving guests formally
vocabulary.example: “Your Grace, members of the Montana and Petrocchi families are awaiting you in the Large Reception Saloon.

vocabulary.example:
”

But it was half an hour before a polite footman came up to the Duke, causing him to flip his pocket flaps down and scatter buttery crumbs in all directions.

vocabulary.definition: to run hurriedly or furtively
vocabulary.example: They heard him scuttle away.

vocabulary.definition: excessively self-satisfied or confident
vocabulary.example: Angelica was inclined to be smug.

vocabulary.definition: in a serious and sincere manner
vocabulary.example: The Duke asked for two bread rolls and two pats of butter and solemnly put one into each pocket.

vocabulary.definition: to walk with long, decisive steps
vocabulary.example: ” And he went striding off there, while Angelica and Tonino jigged against bread and tried not to tread in the butter.

vocabulary.definition: a cunning or deceitful action or scheme
vocabulary.example: “It’s just an enemy trick.

vocabulary.definition: requiring immediate action or attention
vocabulary.example: Tell them it’s urgent.

vocabulary.definition: to talk continuously, especially in a complaining or annoying way
vocabulary.example: Everyone screamed and yammered and said it was an omen of defeat.
